annotations.label: Euclidean space (informal)

anchor.snippet: Euclidean space is the fundamental space of geometry

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` is the standard concrete model; abstract Euclidean vector spaces are `InnerProductSpace ℝ E` with `FiniteDimensional`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness up to isomorphism

anchor.snippet: all Euclidean spaces of a given dimension are isomorphic

annotations.note: Every finite-dimensional real inner product space has a `stdOrthonormalBasis` giving a `LinearIsometryEquiv` with `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)`.

annotations.label: Euclidean vector space

anchor.snippet: A Euclidean vector space is a finite-dimensional inner product space over the real numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib encodes this as `InnerProductSpace ℝ E` together with `FiniteDimensional ℝ E`.

annotations.label: Euclidean space (affine)

anchor.snippet: A Euclidean space is an affine space over the reals such that the associated vector space is a Euclidean vector space

annotations.note: Mathlib models a Euclidean affine space as a `NormedAddTorsor V P` with `V` a finite-dimensional real inner product space (notation `AffineSpace`).

annotations.label: Dimension of a Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: The dimension of a Euclidean space is the dimension of its associated vector space

annotations.note: Dimension of the direction module is `Module.finrank ℝ V`, e.g. `finrank_euclideanSpace_fin` for the standard model.

annotations.label: Points and Euclidean vectors

anchor.snippet: The elements of E are called points

annotations.note: The `AddTorsor V P` structure makes `P` a set of points acted on by translations from the vector group `V`.

annotations.label: Translation action on a point

anchor.snippet: The action of a translation v on a point P provides a point that is denoted P + v

annotations.note: The notation `v +ᵥ P` is the `VAdd` action used in every `AddTorsor`.

annotations.label: Unique displacement vector

anchor.snippet: for every pair of points ( P , Q ) , there is exactly one displacement vector v such that P + v = Q

annotations.note: The existence/uniqueness of `Q -ᵥ P` is built into the `AddTorsor` axioms (`vsub_vadd`, `vadd_vsub`).

annotations.label: Vector space as Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: a Euclidean vector space can be viewed as a Euclidean space that has itself as the associated vector space

annotations.note: Any additive group is canonically an `AddTorsor` over itself, so a Euclidean vector space carries the affine structure tautologically.

annotations.label: Standard Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: A typical case of Euclidean vector space

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` is precisely `ℝⁿ` with the standard inner product, defined via `PiLp 2`.

annotations.label: Isomorphism from a Euclidean frame

anchor.snippet: the choice of a point, called an origin and an orthonormal basis

annotations.note: An `OrthonormalBasis` gives a `LinearIsometryEquiv E ≃ₗᵢ EuclideanSpace ℝ ι`, and combined with `vaddConst` at a chosen origin one gets an `AffineIsometryEquiv`.

annotations.label: Flat / Euclidean subspace

anchor.snippet: A flat , Euclidean subspace or affine subspace of E is a subset F of E such that

annotations.note: `AffineSubspace k P` is exactly the affine-subspace (flat) notion.

annotations.label: Direction of a subspace

anchor.snippet: This linear subspace

annotations.note: `AffineSubspace.direction` returns the linear submodule of displacement vectors of an affine subspace.

annotations.label: Subspace through a point with given direction

anchor.snippet: Conversely, if P is a point of E

annotations.note: `AffineSubspace.mk' p direction` constructs the unique affine subspace through `p` with given direction submodule.

annotations.label: Linear vs Euclidean subspaces

anchor.snippet: Linear subspaces are Euclidean subspaces and a Euclidean subspace is a linear subspace if and only if it contains the zero vector

annotations.note: `Submodule.toAffineSubspace` embeds a linear subspace as an affine subspace containing `0`.

annotations.label: Line in Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: In a Euclidean space, a line is a Euclidean subspace of dimension one

annotations.note: Mathlib does not name `Line` as a separate concept, but a line is realised as `affineSpan ℝ {p, q}` (or any 1-dim `AffineSubspace`).

annotations.label: Line through two points

anchor.snippet: there is exactly one line that passes through (contains) two distinct points

annotations.note: The `affineSpan` of two points is the unique 1-dim flat containing them, but the uniqueness statement is not packaged as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Two distinct lines intersect in at most one point

anchor.snippet: two distinct lines intersect in at most one point

annotations.note: Corollary of line-through-two-points uniqueness; no dedicated lemma found in Mathlib's affine API.

annotations.label: Line segment

anchor.snippet: The line segment , or simply segment , joining the points P and Q

annotations.note: `affineSegment ℝ x y` (and `segment ℝ` for vector spaces) is the line segment between two points.

annotations.label: Parallel subspaces (same dimension)

anchor.snippet: Two subspaces S and T of the same dimension in a Euclidean space are parallel if they have the same direction

annotations.note: `AffineSubspace.Parallel` (notation `∥`) is defined as equality of directions for affine subspaces of the same dimension.

annotations.label: Playfair's axiom

anchor.snippet: there exists exactly one subspace that contains P and is parallel to S

annotations.note: No grep hit for a Playfair-style existence/uniqueness lemma in Mathlib's affine API.

annotations.label: Two lines in a plane meet or are parallel

anchor.snippet: in a Euclidean plane, two lines either meet in one point or are parallel

annotations.note: No statement of this dichotomy for affine lines in a 2-dim space was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parallel subspaces (different dimension)

anchor.snippet: two subspaces are parallel if the direction of one of them is contained in the direction to the other

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Parallel` requires equal directions; the asymmetric weakening for different dimensions is not separately named.

annotations.label: Inner product / dot product notation

anchor.snippet: The inner product of a Euclidean space is often called dot product

annotations.note: Notation `⟪x, y⟫_ℝ` (and `Matrix.dotProduct`) realises the dot product.

annotations.label: Euclidean norm

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean norm of a vector x is

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.norm_eq` shows the norm equals `√(∑ i, ‖x i‖²)`.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: The distance (more precisely the Euclidean distance ) between two points of a Euclidean space is the norm of the translation vector

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.dist_eq` and `dist_eq_norm_vsub` together give the Euclidean distance as `‖P -ᵥ Q‖`.

annotations.label: Length of a segment

anchor.snippet: The length of a segment PQ is the distance d ( P , Q ) between its endpoints P and Q

annotations.note: There is no separate `length` of `affineSegment`, but `dist P Q` plays this role; no dedicated definition was found.

annotations.label: Triangle inequality

anchor.snippet: The distance is a metric , as it is positive definite, symmetric, and satisfies the triangle inequality

annotations.note: `dist_triangle` is the metric-space triangle inequality, applicable to every Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Equality case of triangle inequality

anchor.snippet: the equality is true if and only if a point R belongs to the segment PQ

annotations.note: `dist_add_dist_eq_iff` states `dist a b + dist b c = dist a c ↔ Wbtw ℝ a b c`, the equality case in inner-product space.

annotations.label: Euclidean space is complete

anchor.snippet: every Euclidean space is a complete metric space

annotations.note: Completeness of `PiLp p β` (hence of `EuclideanSpace 𝕜 ι`) is an instance; abstract finite-dim real inner product spaces are also `CompleteSpace`.

annotations.label: Orthogonal vectors

anchor.snippet: are perpendicular or orthogonal if their inner product is zero

annotations.note: Orthogonality is just `⟪x, y⟫ = 0`; the equivalence with `angle = π/2` is `InnerProductGeometry.inner_eq_zero_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two`.

annotations.label: Orthogonal subspaces

anchor.snippet: Two linear subspaces

annotations.note: `Submodule.IsOrtho U V` (notation `U ⟂ V`) means every vector in `U` is orthogonal to every vector in `V`.

annotations.label: Orthogonal Euclidean subspaces / perpendicular lines

anchor.snippet: Two lines, and more generally two Euclidean subspaces

annotations.note: The affine version (perpendicular affine subspaces) is expressed via orthogonality of their `direction`s; no dedicated `AffineSubspace.Perpendicular` was found.

annotations.label: Perpendicular segments / right angle

anchor.snippet: Two segments AB and AC that share a common endpoint A are perpendicular or form a right angle

annotations.note: Right angle is `∠ B A C = π/2`, expressed through `EuclideanGeometry.angle` plus `angle_eq_pi_div_two`-style lemmas.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: This is the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.note: Listed in `docs/100.yaml` as the formalization of the Pythagorean theorem; vector form is `norm_add_sq_real` together with `inner = 0`.

annotations.label: Angle between two vectors

anchor.snippet: The (non-oriented) angle θ between two nonzero vectors x and y

annotations.note: `InnerProductGeometry.angle x y = Real.arccos (⟪x,y⟫ / (‖x‖ * ‖y‖))`.

annotations.label: Angle is well defined via Cauchy–Schwarz

anchor.snippet: By Cauchy–Schwarz inequality , the argument of the arccosine is in the interval

annotations.note: Cauchy–Schwarz appears as `norm_inner_le_norm`; its quotient form `abs_real_inner_div_norm_mul_norm_le_one` shows the arccos argument lies in `[-1, 1]`.

annotations.label: Oriented angle in the plane

anchor.snippet: In an oriented Euclidean plane, one can define the oriented angle of two vectors

annotations.note: `Orientation.oangle` defines the oriented angle in a 2-dim oriented real inner product space.

annotations.label: Angle invariant under positive scaling

anchor.snippet: The angle of two vectors does not change if they are multiplied by positive numbers

annotations.note: `angle_smul_left_of_pos` and `angle_smul_right_of_pos` give invariance of `angle` under positive scalar multiples.

annotations.label: Angle of segments / half-lines

anchor.snippet: the angle of the segments AB and AC is the angle of the vectors

annotations.note: `EuclideanGeometry.angle p₁ p₂ p₃` (notation `∠`) is the angle at `p₂` between `p₂→p₁` and `p₂→p₃`, exactly the segment-angle.

annotations.label: Angle of two lines

anchor.snippet: The angle of two lines is defined as follows

annotations.note: No dedicated `Line.angle` / unsigned angle between two affine lines was found.

annotations.label: Existence of orthonormal basis / Gram–Schmidt

anchor.snippet: Every Euclidean vector space has an orthonormal basis

annotations.note: `stdOrthonormalBasis ℝ E` produces an orthonormal basis of any finite-dim real inner product space; Gram–Schmidt is in `Mathlib.Analysis.InnerProductSpace.GramSchmidtOrtho`.

annotations.label: Cartesian frame

anchor.snippet: a Cartesian frame is a set of data consisting of an orthonormal basis

annotations.note: Mathlib has `OrthonormalBasis` for the linear part; an explicit "Cartesian frame" = origin + orthonormal basis structure is not packaged as a named type.

annotations.label: Cartesian coordinates of a vector

anchor.snippet: The Cartesian coordinates of a vector v

annotations.note: `OrthonormalBasis.repr` is the `LinearIsometryEquiv` sending a vector to its coordinate tuple in `EuclideanSpace`.

annotations.label: Cartesian coordinates of a point

anchor.snippet: The Cartesian coordinates of a point P of E are the Cartesian coordinates of the vector

annotations.note: Point coordinates are obtained as `OrthonormalBasis.repr (P -ᵥ O)`, but there is no single named decl for affine Cartesian coordinates.

annotations.label: Affine / skew coordinates

anchor.snippet: This define affine coordinates , sometimes called skew coordinates

annotations.note: Skew/affine coordinates are realised via `Module.Basis.repr` after picking an origin; not packaged separately.

annotations.label: Affine basis and barycentric coordinates

anchor.snippet: An affine basis of a Euclidean space of dimension n is a set of n + 1 points that are not contained in a hyperplane

annotations.note: `AffineBasis` with `AffineBasis.coord` gives the barycentric coordinates.

annotations.label: Coordinates via homeomorphism

anchor.snippet: Many other coordinates systems can be defined on a Euclidean space E of dimension n

annotations.note: Generic "any homeomorphism with `ℝⁿ` is a coordinate system" is not stated as a Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Polar coordinate system

anchor.snippet: The polar coordinate system (dimension 2) and the spherical and cylindrical coordinate systems (dimension 3) are defined this way

annotations.note: `polarCoord` is an `OpenPartialHomeomorph` between `ℝ²` and `(0,∞) × (-π, π)`; spherical/cylindrical coordinates are not packaged.

annotations.label: Isometry between metric spaces

anchor.snippet: An isometry between two metric spaces is a bijection preserving the distance

annotations.note: `IsometryEquiv` is the type of distance-preserving bijections (`Isometry` for the map alone).

annotations.label: Origin-preserving isometry preserves norm and inner product

anchor.snippet: an isometry that maps the origin to the origin preserves the norm

annotations.note: An origin-preserving isometry between real inner-product spaces is linear (Mazur–Ulam) and `LinearIsometry.inner_map_map` then gives preservation of inner products.

annotations.label: Isometry of Euclidean vector spaces is linear

anchor.snippet: An isometry of Euclidean vector spaces is a linear isomorphism

annotations.note: Mazur–Ulam (in `MazurUlam.lean`) shows isometries of normed real vector spaces are affine; combined with preservation of `0` it gives linearity.

annotations.label: Isometric Euclidean spaces have same dimension

anchor.snippet: This implies that two isometric Euclidean spaces have the same dimension

annotations.note: Follows from `LinearEquiv.finrank_eq` applied to the linear part of an isometry, but not stated as a separate named lemma for Euclidean spaces.

annotations.label: Isometry maps subspaces to subspaces

anchor.snippet: an isometry of Euclidean spaces maps lines to lines

annotations.note: Affine isometries are affine maps, hence send affine subspaces (lines, planes) to affine subspaces; no single named lemma for the geometric statement.

annotations.label: Canonical isometry from origin choice

anchor.snippet: Every point O ∈ E defines an isometry of Euclidean spaces

annotations.note: `AffineIsometryEquiv.vaddConst O` is the canonical isometry `V ≃ᵃⁱ P` defined by `v ↦ O +ᵥ v`.

annotations.label: Isometry via Euclidean frame

anchor.snippet: A Euclidean frame

annotations.note: An origin plus an `OrthonormalBasis` yield an `AffineIsometryEquiv P ≃ EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` by composing `vaddConst` with `OrthonormalBasis.repr`.

annotations.label: Unique Euclidean space of each dimension up to isomorphism

anchor.snippet: up to an isomorphism, there is exactly one Euclidean space of a given dimension

annotations.note: Every finite-dim real inner product space is isometric to `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` via `(stdOrthonormalBasis ℝ E).repr`.

annotations.label: Euclidean group

anchor.snippet: An isometry from a Euclidean space onto itself is called Euclidean isometry

annotations.note: `AffineIsometryEquiv P P` with its group instance is exactly the Euclidean group of `P` (listed in `docs/undergrad.yaml`).

annotations.label: Translation as Euclidean transformation

anchor.snippet: The simplest Euclidean transformations are translations

annotations.note: `AffineIsometryEquiv.constVAdd ℝ P v` is the translation `x ↦ v +ᵥ x` as a self-isometry of `P`.

annotations.label: Translations form a normal subgroup

anchor.snippet: The translations form a normal subgroup of the Euclidean group

annotations.note: No grep hit for the translations subgroup of `AffineIsometryEquiv` being normal.

annotations.label: Associated linear isometry of an isometry

anchor.snippet: A Euclidean isometry f of a Euclidean space E defines a linear isometry

annotations.note: `AffineIsometryEquiv.linearIsometryEquiv` extracts the linear part of an affine isometry.

annotations.label: Homomorphism onto orthogonal group

anchor.snippet: is a group homomorphism from the Euclidean group onto the group of linear isometries

annotations.note: The map `AffineIsometryEquiv → LinearIsometryEquiv` exists and is a group hom, but surjectivity is not stated as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Stabilizer isomorphic to orthogonal group

anchor.snippet: The isometries that fix a given point P form the stabilizer subgroup

annotations.note: No lemma identifying the stabilizer of a point in the Euclidean group with the orthogonal group was found.

annotations.label: Euclidean group as semidirect product

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean group is the semidirect product of the translation group and the orthogonal group

annotations.note: No semidirect-product decomposition of the Euclidean group was found.

annotations.label: Special Euclidean group / displacements

anchor.snippet: The special orthogonal group is the normal subgroup of the orthogonal group that preserves handedness

annotations.note: Although `Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup` exists, the special Euclidean group (orientation-preserving isometries of `P`) is not packaged.

annotations.label: Reflections

anchor.snippet: Typical examples of rigid transformations that are not rigid motions are reflections

annotations.note: `Module.reflection` is the linear reflection, and `EuclideanGeometry.reflection` (in `Geometry/Euclidean/Projection.lean`) defines reflection across an affine subspace.

annotations.label: Non-motion equals reflection times motion

anchor.snippet: every rigid transformation that is not a rigid motion is the product of r and a rigid motion

annotations.note: No such decomposition statement was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean topology

anchor.snippet: This topology is called the Euclidean topology

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` carries the standard `TopologicalSpace`/`MetricSpace` instance inherited from `PiLp 2`.

annotations.label: Euclidean topology equals product topology

anchor.snippet: this topology is also the product topology

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.equiv` is a continuous linear equivalence between `EuclideanSpace 𝕜 ι` and `ι → 𝕜`, so the `PiLp 2` topology agrees with the product topology.

annotations.label: Open sets via open balls

anchor.snippet: The open sets are the subsets that contains an open ball around each of their points

annotations.note: `Metric.isOpen_iff` characterises open sets in any metric space by containing an open ball around each point.

annotations.label: Topological dimension equals dimension

anchor.snippet: The topological dimension of a Euclidean space equals its dimension

annotations.note: Topological (covering) dimension of `ℝⁿ` is not formalized; only `Module.finrank` and Hausdorff dimension are available.

annotations.label: Invariance of domain

anchor.snippet: the theorem of invariance of domain asserts that a subset of a Euclidean space is open

annotations.note: Grep finds no `invariance_of_domain` or Brouwer-style statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heine–Borel: closed and bounded is compact

anchor.snippet: Euclidean spaces are complete and locally compact

annotations.note: `Metric.isCompact_iff_isClosed_bounded` is the Heine–Borel theorem for proper metric spaces, which includes finite-dim real inner product spaces.

annotations.label: Equivalence of axiomatic definitions (Artin)

anchor.snippet: Emil Artin has proved that all these definitions of a Euclidean space are equivalent

annotations.note: Artin's meta-theorem comparing synthetic vs analytic axiomatizations of Euclidean geometry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Manifold (locally Euclidean)

anchor.snippet: A manifold is a space that in the neighborhood of each point resembles a Euclidean space

annotations.note: `ChartedSpace H M` axiomatises a space locally modelled on `H`; specialising to `H = EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` gives a topological manifold.

annotations.label: Pseudo-Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: A pseudo-Euclidean space is an affine space with an associated real vector space equipped with a non-degenerate quadratic form

annotations.note: Non-degenerate quadratic forms exist in Mathlib (`QuadraticMap.Nondegenerate`), but no named `PseudoEuclideanSpace` structure was found.

annotations.label: Minkowski space

anchor.snippet: A fundamental example of such a space is the Minkowski space

annotations.note: Grep finds no `MinkowskiSpace` or Lorentzian-signature definition in Mathlib.
